---
title: "Bear Cub Mining Claim, Cape Nome — historical placer drilling reconstructed"
subtitle: "Four operators, 35 years, twenty-four paper drill logs into a structured 3-D resource model"
date: 2026-05-11
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
---

## The experiment

Bear Cub Mining Claim is a four-patent placer claim on the Nome
paleo-beach gold deposits in western Alaska, owned by the family
since the early twentieth century. Its drilling history covers three
operators across four form-types between 1919 and 1955, producing
twenty-four paper drill logs that had never been transcribed. The
logs encode interval-by-interval ground character, color counts of
visible gold particles per panned bailer sample, field-estimated
weights, supervisor-corrected bench-scale weights, and (in later
revisions) a green-pencil reprice of the historical pennyweight values
at the modern gold price. The goal of this project was to take what
had been paper-only data sitting in a banker's box for forty years
and turn it into a structured geospatial dataset with quantified
resource estimates, a three-dimensional model of the bedrock surface
and pay zone, and a defensible chain of title back to the original
1908 federal mineral patent.

![Bear Cub MS 1178 on satellite imagery. Twenty-four drill holes color-coded by pay-zone average grade.](../../data/derived/bear_cub_resource/fig_aerial_pay_zone_grade.png)

## Proving title

The claim is BLM Mineral Survey 1178 in the Cape Nome mining district,
southern Seward Peninsula. Title rests on three documents that
together pin the property to a specific footprint in WGS84 coordinates
and a specific legal owner of record:

- The **1908 federal mineral patent** (issued under the General Mining
  Law of 1872 to the original locator) carries the surveyor's
  metes-and-bounds course descriptions for the patent boundary.
- The **2014 BLM re-survey** (Scott McClintock, RLS 8904-3)
  re-located the four boundary monuments under modern geodesy and
  produced the four-corner WGS84 coordinates that anchor every other
  geospatial layer in this project.
- The **Alaska state recorder's records** for the Nome recording
  district carry the chain of conveyances from the original patentee
  to the current family ownership.

Federal mineral patents are perpetual property under the General
Mining Law; the claim does not require annual maintenance work or
rental fees the way an unpatented claim does. The four-corner
reconstruction walks the surveyor's metes-and-bounds language to
within 0.3 meters of the McClintock 2014 corner monuments — the
round-trip residual on the affine transform that takes the historical
local-feet survey grid to modern WGS84.

## The OCR pipeline and dealing with messy real-world data

The twenty-four logs span four distinct form-types from three
operators: 1919 Frozen Ground drill reports (per-2-foot intervals,
gold weights inline), 1925 and 1936 Hammon Consolidated prospect
drilling field logs (some with weights on the front page, others
with sample-aggregated weights on a back page that has to be
distributed back across intervals), and a single 1955 Alaska Gold
log. The handwriting style, column layout, units (milligrams in some
logs, grains in others, pennyweights-per-yard in others), and
bedrock-call convention all differ across operators and across
decades.

![Source-log example. L2 H4, 1919 Frozen Ground report — the largest single-hole hit in the corpus. Per-2-foot intervals down the left; the WEIGHT column entries on the last 13-15 feet (the bedrock-contact pay zone) total 470+ mg, the largest single-hole hit in the 24-hole corpus.](../../data/raw/bear_cub/page_pngs/L2 H4__p1.png)

The pipeline uses multiple OCR engines in series with a final
arbitration step:

- **Tesseract** handles printed form headers (operator name,
  drill-rig type, hole number) where the layout is typewritten.
- **Hugging Face TrOCR** handles depth and lithology columns where
  the handwriting is the original driller's.
- **Google Cloud Vision** handles the red-pencil supervisor
  corrections layered on top of the original ink, which Tesseract
  and TrOCR routinely misread because they are designed for
  single-pass clean handwriting, not for an ink-then-red-pencil-
  then-green-pencil palimpsest.
- **Claude Opus 4.5 with structured-output JSON** is the arbitration
  stage. The four upstream engines each produce a candidate
  transcription per cell; Claude is given all four candidates plus
  the column's expected unit (milligrams, feet, inches) and emits
  the single most plausible value with a confidence flag.

The bigger challenge than OCR per se was schema-level: the four
operator-specific conventions disagree about what the same field on
the same physical form actually means. Convention C (the 1936 Hammon
field log) records weights only at the sample level on a back page;
the front page carries per-interval color counts but no weights.
Without recovering the back-page sample totals and distributing them
back across intervals using a color-weighted proportional allocation,
every Convention C hole comes back at zero grade and the
property-total resource is roughly halved. The pipeline implements
the sample-recovery rule as a separate post-OCR step. The full
breakdown of how the historical logs encode their data lives at
[research/bear_cub_placer_drill_log_anatomy.md](../../research/bear_cub_placer_drill_log_anatomy.md).

A cross-check stage projects every transcribed hole onto a 1936
cartographer's plat of the property; each transcribed local-grid
coordinate has to land on its annotated dot on the plat to within a
few feet. Five OCR errors the model had self-rated as high confidence
turned up only because the holes plotted in the wrong place — the
OCR was confident and wrong, and the geospatial cross-check was the
only thing that caught it.

## Algorithms for yield and pay-zone grade

The standard placer-evaluation approach for a small-claim property is
a polygon block model. We tessellate the property's four-corner
quadrilateral into Voronoi cells around the drill-hole positions:
each cell is the region of the patent closer to a particular drill
hole than to any other drill hole. The cell's grade is taken as
constant and equal to that hole's per-interval grade, depth-weighted
across the cell's vertical column. Multiplying cell area by depth
gives a volume; multiplying volume by grade gives ounces; summing
over cells gives a property-total resource. Two variants of the
column definition:

- **Full drilled column** — surface-to-bedrock depth; the
  conservative estimate that includes overburden.
- **Pay zone** — restricts to the bedrock-contact horizon (typically
  the last ten to twenty feet above bedrock, where placer gold
  concentrates in the third-beach-line paleo-shoreline); the
  higher-grade-per-yard estimate that historic operators quoted.

![Pay-zone Voronoi cells on the local-feet grid, colored by pay-zone average grade. The southern + south-central holes show the highest concentrations.](../../data/derived/bear_cub_resource/fig_pay_zone_grade_map.png)

![Cross-hole grade fence diagram, reading west to east. Bedrock-contact pay (3rd beach line) shows in the dark cells immediately above the brown bar at L2 H4, L6500 H6554, L6900 H6952, and L7300 H7350.](../../data/derived/bear_cub_resource/fig_grade_fence.png)

The point estimate hides three real sources of uncertainty:

- **Gold fineness** — Bear Cub gold averages 89 percent fine but
  assays vary across the Nome district between 85 and 92 percent.
- **Per-milligram OCR-and-transcription noise** — the manual-review
  stage cannot fully eliminate it; calibrated at about 5 percent
  multiplicative error.
- **Bit-area uncertainty** — the casing-versus-bit distinction
  introduces a 5 percent error on the swept-area calculation.

A Monte Carlo wrapper around the polygon estimator samples each of
these distributions per iteration: fineness uniform on [0.85, 0.92],
per-milligram error normal with 5 percent standard deviation, bit
area normal with 5 percent standard deviation. One thousand samples
give the resource estimate's 90 percent credible interval.

![Monte Carlo distribution of the pay-zone-only resource estimate over 1,000 samples. The 90% credible interval is narrow because per-hole errors average out across 24 holes.](../../data/derived/bear_cub_resource/fig_mc_uncertainty.png)

The three-dimensional model takes each surveyed drill-hole collar,
walks the casing-depth column downward through the gravel cover, and
produces a PyVista mesh of the implied bedrock surface and the
pay-zone horizon. The fence-diagram visualization is the same data
in profile view, with bedrock-contact pay cells (the dark band
immediately above the bedrock surface) showing the third-beach-line
geometry the historic operators were chasing.

![3-D drill-hole model with bedrock surface and pay-zone horizon, oblique view.](../../data/derived/bear_cub_resource/fig_3d_oblique.png)

![Plan-view 3-D, holes colored by pay-zone average grade.](../../data/derived/bear_cub_resource/fig_3d_plan.png)

A Gaussian-process regression on (x, y) → predicted pay-zone grade
produces a continuous prospectivity surface across the patent
quadrilateral, with a per-location uncertainty estimate from the GP's
posterior variance. With only twenty-four holes the spatial-only GP
does not predict grade accurately enough to use as a primary resource
model (negative leave-one-out R² because pay-zone grade has a sharp
gradient at the beach line), but the same GP's posterior-variance
output drives a drill-site recommender that selects the next hole
position to maximize either predictive uncertainty (pure information
gain) or expected improvement against a 0.05 ounce-per-cubic-yard
grade target.

![Gaussian-process predicted grade surface from 24 holes.](../../data/derived/bear_cub_resource/fig_gp_predicted_grade_surface.png)

![Drill-site recommender: next-hole picks by expected improvement and predictive variance. Both outputs are visualizations the family can act on if they decide to drill additional holes.](../../data/derived/bear_cub_resource/fig_gp_drill_recommender.png)

## Interesting findings

### Historical operator-aggregate numbers disagree with the modern analysis in ways the methodology explains

The published 1962 operator estimate (William J. Tweet, Bear Cub
Mining Company) puts the property total at 10,056 ounces. Our
pay-zone polygon block estimate produces a 90-percent credible
interval that sits well below that number. The gap is methodological,
not a measurement disagreement: Tweet's number applies a generous
pay-zone definition that includes intervals our cleaner per-interval
grade column reads as non-pay, and the historic estimate combines
the polygon block result with a separate trench-survey number from
areas not covered by the drill grid. We document the divergence with
the actual methodology variants and show where each operator's number
lands. This is the most defensible thing the project produces,
because it explains where every published number for the claim's
resource came from and what assumption set produced it.

### The pipeline is portable to the rest of the Nome paleo-beach archive

The same multi-engine OCR plus schema-aware Claude arbitration plus
geospatial cross-check pipeline generalizes to:

- The other three family-owned claims (estimated several hundred
  unscanned drill logs across the Janin and Huntington operating
  eras of the 1920s through 1940s)
- The broader corpus of Nome dark-data archive material — court
  filings, surveyors' notes, family correspondence, mill tailings
  logs, clean-up sheets — that has been sitting in physical
  archives for decades.

The most immediately actionable extension is to apply the existing
pipeline to the Janin Huntington archive logs as the next OCR
target. The longer-arc extensions are three concrete spinoff
projects:

- A **surveyor's toolkit** that takes a few field GPS readings plus
  a patent description and anchors the local survey grid in WGS84
  (the four-corner affine and BLM-patent-walker code from Bear Cub
  is the seed).
- A **drill-log command-line tool** that ingests a folder of
  historical mining-record scans and produces a structured CSV with
  provenance and confidence flags per cell.
- A **dark-data archive ingestion framework** — the same approach
  applied to court filings, surveyors' notes, title histories, and
  family correspondence around a mining property, pulled into a
  single linked dataset with full provenance.

## Sources

- BLM, 2014. McClintock Re-Survey of MS 1178 (Bear Cub Placer Claim, Cape Nome mining district), Scott McClintock RLS 8904-3.
- Reed, I. McK., 1908. Patent plat for MS 1178.
- Tweet, W. J., 1962. Resource estimate, Bear Cub Mining Company.
- Wells, J. H., 1969. *Placer Examination: Principles and Practice*. BLM Tech. Bull. 4.
- Wolff, E. N., 1964/1969. *Handbook for the Alaskan Prospector*. UAF Mineral Industry Research Laboratory.
